t: How many lines of symmetry does a regular hexagon have? More or fewer than a square?
e: A regular hexagon has 6 lines of symmetry. A square has 4. So the hexagon has MORE lines of symmetry.

t: Can you fold a rectangle in half to make two squares? When is this possible?
o: Only when the rectangle is exactly twice as long as it is wide
e: You can fold a rectangle into 2 squares ONLY when the length is exactly twice the width. Then each half is a perfect square.

t: Mia says cutting a square corner-to-corner gives 2 right triangles. She also says each triangle has 2 lines of symmetry. Is she fully correct?
o: The first part is correct, but each triangle has only 1 line of symmetry
The first part is correct, but each triangle has 0 lines of symmetry
e: Mia is right that cutting diagonally gives 2 right triangles. But each is an isosceles right triangle with only 1 line of symmetry (along its own line from the right angle to the hypotenuse midpoint).

t: Ben says all shapes with 4 lines of symmetry must be squares. Can you find a counterexample?
o: He is correct -- only squares have 4 lines
No, but a circle has more
Yes -- a regular octagon also has more than 4
The plus sign (+) shape has 4 lines and is not a square
e: A plus sign (+) shape has 4 lines of symmetry but is not a square. Ben is wrong -- other shapes can have 4 lines of symmetry too.

t: You cut a regular hexagon from center to each corner. How many triangles do you get, and what kind are they?
e: Cutting from center to each of the 6 corners makes 6 equilateral triangles. Each has 3 equal sides.

t: A shape is made from 4 identical squares arranged in a T-shape. How many lines of symmetry does it have?
e: A T-shaped tetromino has exactly 1 line of symmetry -- a vertical line down the center of the top of the T. It is not symmetric left-right if flipped another way.

t: Sam says: 'If I fold a shape in half and the pieces do not match, the shape has no symmetry.' Is he correct?
o: Yes, that fold proves no symmetry
No -- he might have folded along the wrong line
Yes -- you only need to try one fold
e: Sam is wrong. He may have folded along a line that is not a line of symmetry. He needs to try ALL possible fold lines before concluding there is no symmetry.

t: You combine a square and a triangle (sharing one full side). How many sides does the new shape have?
e: A square has 4 sides and a triangle has 3 sides = 7 total. But they share 1 side (hidden inside): 7 - 2 = 5 outside sides.

t: A regular polygon has the same number of lines of symmetry as it has sides. How many lines of symmetry does a regular octagon have?
e: A regular octagon has 8 sides, so it has 8 lines of symmetry. Regular polygons always have as many lines of symmetry as sides.

t: Rosa puts 2 regular hexagons together sharing one full side. How many sides does the new shape have?
e: Each hexagon has 6 sides = 12 total. They share 1 side, removing 2 edges from the outside: 12 - 2 = 10 sides.

t: Jaya says a parallelogram (not a rectangle) has 2 lines of symmetry. Is she correct?
o: No, a non-rectangular parallelogram has 0 lines of symmetry
e: A parallelogram that is not a rectangle has 0 lines of symmetry. No fold line will make the two halves match. (It does have rotational symmetry, but no line symmetry.)

t: You have 12 identical equilateral triangles. What is the largest regular polygon you could tile with them?
e: You cannot tile a square or octagon with equilateral triangles. But 6 equilateral triangles make a regular hexagon, and 12 make 2 hexagons or a larger hexagonal shape.

t: Kai cuts a rectangle diagonally both ways (making an X). How many pieces does he get, and what shape are they?
e: Two diagonal cuts through a rectangle make 4 triangles. If the rectangle is not a square, you get 2 pairs: 2 wider triangles and 2 taller triangles.

t: How many total lines of symmetry do a square and an equilateral triangle have COMBINED?
e: A square has 4 lines of symmetry. An equilateral triangle has 3. Combined: 4 + 3 = 7.

t: Alex says you can make a hexagon by arranging 3 rhombuses around a center point. Is this possible?
o: Yes, 3 identical rhombuses can tile a regular hexagon
e: Yes! Three identical rhombuses (each made of 2 equilateral triangles) can be arranged around a center to form a regular hexagon.

t: A shape has exactly 2 lines of symmetry. Which of these could it be?
e: A non-square rectangle has exactly 2 lines of symmetry (horizontal and vertical). The triangle has 3, hexagon has 6, pentagon has 5.

t: You remove 1 small square from the corner of a big square. How many sides does the remaining shape have?
e: Removing a corner square creates a notch. The new shape has 6 sides: the 3 remaining big square sides, plus 2 small edges from the cut, plus 1 remaining edge.

t: Maya says a circle has exactly 1 line of symmetry. Leo says it has infinitely many. Who is correct?
e: Leo is correct. ANY line through the center of a circle is a line of symmetry. There are infinitely many such lines.

t: You compose a new shape using 1 square and 2 equilateral triangles (one on the top side, one on the bottom side). How many sides does it have?
e: The square has 4 sides. Each triangle adds 2 new sides but covers 1 square side. Total: 4 - 2 (covered) + 4 (new triangle sides) = 6 sides.

t: What is the fewest number of lines of symmetry any regular polygon can have?
e: The simplest regular polygon is an equilateral triangle, which has 3 lines of symmetry. No regular polygon has fewer than 3, because the fewest sides a polygon can have is 3.
